notes.anchor: when Laevus and Nerva left me
notes.note: Laevus and Nerva were the legates (legati) carrying Plancus's previous dispatch back to Rome; they appear only here and are otherwise unidentified. Cicero refers Plancus to their eyewitness account of his deliberations.

notes.anchor: the inconstancy and unreliability of Lepidus's army
notes.note: Lepidus's troops were riddled with Caesarian sympathy for Antony; Plancus rightly fears they will go over, as they soon did, dragging Lepidus into open alliance with Antony at the end of May 43 BC.

notes.anchor: to wait at the Is\`ere until Brutus brought his army across
notes.note: The Brutus here is Decimus Brutus Albinus, who after the relief of Mutina was marching from Cisalpine Gaul to join Plancus; the cautious plan was to await his crossing before engaging Antony. The two never effectively combined, and Decimus was killed fleeing Gaul later in 43 BC.

A sneer at P. Ventidius Bassus's low birth: tradition held he had begun life driving mules and supplying transport for armies. He nonetheless brought three legions to Antony and later, ironically, won Rome's great victories over the Parthians.

notes.anchor: the bridge, however, which I had built across the Is\`ere I have left in place, with two forts set at its heads
notes.note: Plancus kept his pontoon bridge over the Isère (Latin Isara) garrisoned with twin bridgehead forts (castella ad capita) so that Decimus Brutus's army could cross without delay; standard Roman practice for securing a river passage.
